text: Scarless, entirely laparoscopic panproctolectomy and extraspondyric dissection with perineal extraction of the specimen for cancer of anorectal junction arising on the background of long-standing ulcerative colitis. A sixty-six-year-old male with a background history of long-standing ulcerative colitis underwent flexible sigmoidoscopy for a flare-up of symptoms. This found largely normal mucosa but a fibropithelial polyp at the anorectal junction. No synchronous lesions were detected on colonoscopy. Histology from the biopsy showed high-grade dysplasia with some features raising the possibility of deeper invasion. There was no evidence of metastatic disease on staging CT. MRI rectum could not visualize the primary tumor and gave overall TNM-A stage DXN-M-M. After discussion and multidisciplinary meeting, the patient proceeded to surgery.

Scarless, entirely laparoscopic panproctolectomy and extraspondyric dissection with perineal extraction of the specimen for cancer of anorectal junction arising on the background of long-standing ulcerative colitis. A sixty-six-year-old male with a background history of long-standing ulcerative colitis underwent flexible sigmoidoscopy for a flare-up of symptoms. This found largely normal mucosa but a fibropithelial polyp at the anorectal junction. No synchronous lesions were detected on colonoscopy. Histology from the biopsy showed high-grade dysplasia with some features raising the possibility of deeper invasion. There was no evidence of metastatic disease on staging CT. MRI rectum could not visualize the primary tumor and gave overall TNM-A stage DXN-M-M. After discussion and multidisciplinary meeting, the patient proceeded to surgery.

The surgical setup includes five laparoscopic ports: three 12mm ports located in the umbilical region, right iliac fossa, and left hypochondrium, and two 5mm ports positioned in the right hypochondrium and left iliac fossa.

The surgical setup includes five laparoscopic ports: three 12mm ports located in the umbilical region, right iliac fossa, and left hypochondrium, and two 5mm ports positioned in the right hypochondrium and left iliac fossa.

The surgeon stands on the left side of the patient, using ports in the left hypochondrium and left iliac fossa to mobilize the right colon. After establishing pneumoperitoneum, a medial to lateral dissection of the right colonic mesentery is performed. The ileocolic pedicle is identified, clipped, and divided with hemolog clips. The ileocolic vessels are ligated at a mid-mesenteric level, and the terminal ileal mesentery is divided, allowing the terminal ileum to be divided with a stapler.

The surgeon stands on the left side of the patient and uses the ports in the left hypochondrium and left iliac fossa to mobilize the right colon. After establishing pneumoperitoneum, a medial to lateral dissection of the right colonic mesentery is performed. The ileocolic pedicle is identified, clipped, and divided using two hemolog clips. The ileocolic vessels are ligated at a mid-mesenteric level, and the terminal ileal mesentery is divided, allowing the terminal ileum to be divided with a stapler.

The surgeon moves caudally and uses ports in the right and left iliac fossa to access the transverse colon. The gastrocolic ligament is divided to enter the lesser sac, allowing for the mobilization of the transverse colon. The mesentery of the transverse colon is dissected to approach the middle colic vessels.

The surgeon moves caudally and uses the ports in the right and left iliac fossa to access the transverse colon. The gastrocolic ligament is divided to enter the lesser sac, allowing for the mobilization of the transverse colon. The dissection of the mesentery of the transverse colon is performed to approach the middle colic vessels.
The surgeon divides multiple lymphatic and venous branches before ligating and dividing the middle colic artery during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon divides multiple lymphatic and venous branches, followed by the ligation and division of the middle colic artery.

The surgeon switches to the right side of the patient, using ports in the right hypochondrium and iliac fossa to approach the splenic flexure. The left colon and rectum are mobilized through supracolic dissection, with careful movements to safely navigate around the splenic flexure and avoid iatrogenic splenic injury.

The surgeon switches to the right side of the patient, using ports in the right hypochondrium and iliac fossa to approach the splenic flexure. The left colon and rectum are mobilized through supracolic dissection, with careful movements to safely mobilize the colon and avoid iatrogenic splenic injury.
The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric artery and performs a medial to lateral dissection, culminating in the high ligation of the artery.

The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric artery and performs a medial to lateral dissection, culminating in the high ligation of the artery. The laparoscopic procedure continues with the mobilization of the rectum, dividing the peritoneal reflection and proceeding in the plane of total mesorectal excision to reach the middle to low rectal canal.

The surgeon completes the laparoscopic procedure by mobilizing the rectum. The peritoneal reflection is divided, and the dissection continues in the plane of total mesorectal excision, reaching the middle to low rectal canal.
The laparoscopic view shows the perineal dissection being performed in a codocranial direction. The specimen is then extracted through the perineal wound.
The laparoscopic view shows the perineal dissection being performed in a codocranial direction. The specimen is then extracted through the perineal wound.
The surgeon closes the pelvic peritoneum over a suction drain using an intracorporeal continuous suture technique.
The pelvic peritoneum is closed over a suction drain using an intracorporeal continuous suture.
The surgeon completes the operation by forming an ileostomy through the incision for one of the ports in the right iliac fossa. The visual content shows the creation of the ileostomy site.
The surgeon completes the operation by forming an ileostomy through the incision for one of the ports in the right iliac fossa. The visual content shows the final steps of the ileostomy formation.

The patient recovered well, and follow-up CT at six months found no evidence of recurrent disease. Histology showed polypoid low- and high-grade dysplasia with evidence of focal superficial submucosal invasion, in keeping with a well-to-moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. In summary, this case shows that in experienced hands, panproctocolectomy can be achieved laparoscopically with specimen extraction through natural orifices.
